summary:
Obstetrics & Childbirth

Anatomic & Physiologic Changes in Pregnancy

Pregnancy causes profound changes in virtually every organ system. Understanding these changes is essential for recognizing complications and providing appropriate care. Key changes include:

  • Uterus: Enlarges from ~70 g to ~1,100 g, rising from the pelvis to the level of the xiphoid process by term. The enlarged uterus can compress the inferior vena cava when the patient is supine, causing supine hypotensive syndrome (see below).
  • Cardiovascular: Blood volume increases by 30–50% (plasma increase > RBC increase, creating a physiologic "dilutional" anemia). Cardiac output increases by 30–50%. Heart rate increases by 10–20 bpm. Blood pressure typically decreases in the second trimester and returns to baseline in the third.
  • Respiratory: The diaphragm is elevated by the enlarging uterus, reducing functional residual capacity (FRC) by 20–25%. Minute ventilation increases by 30–50% due to increased tidal volume (progesterone effect). Pregnant patients have a higher baseline respiratory rate and lower CO₂ reserve — they desaturate more rapidly during apnea.
  • Gastrointestinal: Decreased gastric motility and prolonged gastric emptying time due to progesterone. The lower esophageal sphincter is relaxed, increasing the risk of gastroesophageal reflux and aspiration. The gravid uterus displaces the stomach upward and posteriorly.
  • Renal: Increased renal blood flow and GFR (50% increase). The ureters are dilated by progesterone and compressed by the uterus, increasing the risk of urinary stasis and pyelonephritis.
  • Musculoskeletal: Relaxin hormone causes ligamentous relaxation, particularly in the pelvis and sacroiliac joints, to facilitate vaginal delivery. This also increases the risk of falls and joint injury.
Supine Hypotensive Syndrome: When a pregnant patient (especially >20 weeks) lies supine, the gravid uterus compresses the inferior vena cava, reducing venous return to the heart by up to 30%, causing hypotension, pallor, diaphoresis, nausea, and dizziness. Treatment: Immediately roll the patient onto their LEFT side (or place a folded towel/blanket under the right hip to tilt the pelvis 15–30°). This displaces the uterus off the IVC and restores cardiac output. NEVER transport a pregnant patient flat on their back.

Stages of Labor

Labor is the process by which the fetus, placenta, and membranes are expelled through the birth canal. It is divided into four stages:

StageDurationDescriptionEMS provider Role
1st Stage — DilationVariable (hours); typically longest stage. Primiparous: 8–12 h. Multiparous: 4–8 h.Onset of regular contractions to full dilation of the cervix (10 cm). Contractions become stronger, more frequent, and closer together. The amniotic sac may rupture spontaneously ("water breaking").Assess frequency and duration of contractions. Determine if delivery is imminent (crowning, urge to push). Transport if delivery is NOT imminent. Keep patient comfortable.
2nd Stage — Delivery30 min to 2 h (may be shorter in multiparous)Full cervical dilation (10 cm) to delivery of the baby. The mother feels an uncontrollable urge to push. The presenting part descends through the birth canal and delivers.Prepare for delivery if birth is imminent. Assist with controlled delivery of the head, suction, check for nuchal cord, deliver shoulders, and clamp/cut the cord after pulsations cease.
3rd Stage — Placenta5–30 min after delivery of babyDelivery of the placenta and fetal membranes. Signs of placental separation include: a gush of blood from the vagina, lengthening of the umbilical cord, and the uterus becoming firm and globular (rising in the abdomen).DO NOT pull on the cord to expedite placental delivery. Allow spontaneous delivery. Place the placenta in a plastic bag and transport it with the patient for hospital inspection of completeness.
4th Stage — Recovery1–2 hours after deliveryImmediate postpartum period. The uterus contracts to control bleeding at the placental site. Close monitoring for postpartum hemorrhage is essential.Continue fundal massage to promote uterine contraction. Monitor vital signs, vaginal bleeding, and uterine tone. Transport for ongoing monitoring.

Normal Delivery Steps

When delivery is imminent (crowning, mother feels urge to push, contractions 1–2 minutes apart, or the presenting part is visible), prepare for field delivery. Never delay transport for delivery unless birth is imminent — if you can see the presenting part and delivery is clearly about to happen, stay and deliver.

  1. Position: Place the mother on a firm surface in a supine position with knees bent and apart (lithotomy position). Drape for privacy and to create a clean field.
  2. Sterile field: Open the OB delivery kit. Place sterile towels under the mother's buttocks and over the abdomen. Wear sterile gloves, gown, mask, and eye protection.
  3. Control the head: As the head delivers (occiput anterior, face toward the mother's spine), place one hand on the top of the baby's head and apply gentle pressure to control the speed of delivery. Use the other hand to support the perineum. Do NOT touch the baby's face or eyes.
  4. Check for nuchal cord: As the head delivers, gently slip your finger along the baby's neck to check if the umbilical cord is wrapped around it. If a nuchal cord is present and loose, gently reduce it over the baby's head. If tight and cannot be reduced, clamp the cord in two places and cut between the clamps, then unwrap.
  5. Suction: After the head delivers and before the body delivers, suction the baby's mouth first, then the nose (using a bulb syringe). Suctioning the mouth first prevents aspiration of meconium-stained fluid if the baby gasps when the nose is stimulated. Suction each nostril separately.
  6. Deliver the shoulders: The baby's head will rotate spontaneously to one side (restitution). Gently guide the head downward to deliver the anterior shoulder (under the pubic symphysis), then upward to deliver the posterior shoulder. Do NOT pull on the head or neck — excessive traction can cause brachial plexus injury (Erb-Duchenne palsy).
  7. Deliver the body: After both shoulders deliver, the rest of the body follows easily. Support the baby with both hands — one supporting the head and neck, the other supporting the body. The baby will be slippery — be prepared. Have a warm, dry towel ready.
  8. Clamp and cut the cord: After the cord stops pulsating (usually 1–3 minutes after delivery), place two clamps approximately 2–3 inches apart, 6–8 inches from the baby's abdomen. Cut between the clamps with sterile scissors. Do NOT milk the cord — this can cause a rapid fluid shift and polycythemia.
  9. Dry, warm, and stimulate: Immediately dry the baby with a warm towel, remove the wet towel, and place the baby skin-to-skin on the mother's chest or in a warm, dry blanket. Cover the baby's head with a hat to prevent heat loss. Stimulate the baby to breathe by drying and rubbing the back.
  10. APGAR scoring: Perform APGAR scoring at 1 minute and 5 minutes after birth (see below).
Clinical Pearl — Suction Sequence: Always suction the mouth FIRST, then the nose. If you suction the nose first, the baby may gasp and aspirate any fluid in the oropharynx. Suction gently — over-stimulation of the posterior pharynx can cause vagal response and bradycardia.

APGAR Scoring

The APGAR score is a rapid assessment tool used to evaluate the newborn's transition to extrauterine life at 1 minute and 5 minutes after birth. A score of 7–10 is reassuring, 4–6 indicates moderate depression requiring intervention, and <7 requires intervention. Each of five categories is scored 0, 1, or 2 (maximum total: 10):

Component0 Points1 Point2 Points
Appearance (Skin color)Pale or cyanotic (blue all over)Acrocyanotic (body pink, extremities blue)Pink all over (normal)
Pulse (Heart rate)Absent<100 bpm≥100 bpm
Grimace (Reflex irritability)No response to stimulationGrimace or weak cry when stimulatedStrong cry, cough, or sneeze
Activity (Muscle tone)Limp, floppySome flexion of extremitiesActive motion, well-flexed
Respiration (Breathing)Absent (apneic)Slow, irregular, weak cryStrong cry, normal rate and rhythm

APGAR < 7 at 1 minute indicates need for intervention: stimulate, dry, warm, clear airway, and provide oxygen. APGAR < 7 at 5 minutes indicates significant depression and a higher risk of neurologic impairment — initiate resuscitation per NRP algorithm. The 5-minute score is more predictive of long-term outcomes than the 1-minute score.

Abnormal Delivery Complications

Breech Presentation

Occurs when the fetus presents buttocks or feet first instead of head first (3–4% of term deliveries). Types: frank breech (buttocks first, legs extended up), complete breech (buttocks first, legs crossed), and footling breech (one or both feet first). DO NOT pull on the breech — pulling can cause the arms to extend above the head (nuchal arms) or cause cervical spine injury. Management: support the presenting part with a towel, create a "hammock" for the buttocks, and transport emergently. If the body delivers but the head is trapped, the head may compress the cord against the pelvic inlet — maintain the baby's airway by keeping the baby's face down and inserting a gloved hand into the vagina to create an airway (the "posterior hand" or "face-to-pubes" maneuver). Transport with the towel-supported breech — do not attempt to extract the baby.

Prolapsed Umbilical Cord

The umbilical cord prolapses through the cervix and lies in the vagina ahead of the presenting part. Contractions compress the cord against the fetal head or pelvic bones, cutting off fetal oxygenation. This is a life-threatening emergency for the fetus. Management:

  • Place the mother in knee-chest position (kneeling with chest on the stretcher, buttocks elevated) or extreme Trendelenburg (head down) to use gravity to reduce cord compression
  • Insert a gloved hand into the vagina and gently elevate the presenting part off the cord (hold it up with two fingers) — maintain this position until delivery
  • Keep the cord moist and warm by covering it with a sterile saline-soaked towel — a dry, cold cord develops vasospasm, reducing oxygen delivery
  • Do NOT attempt to push the cord back into the uterus — this worsens prolapse
  • Transport emergently — this patient needs immediate cesarean delivery
Meconium Staining

Meconium (fetal stool) in the amniotic fluid indicates the fetus may have experienced hypoxia or stress in utero, causing relaxation of the anal sphincter and passage of meconium. Meconium-stained fluid appears green or brown. The baby may aspirate meconium during delivery, causing meconium aspiration syndrome (chemical pneumonitis, airway obstruction, respiratory distress). If meconium is present and the baby is depressed (poor tone, not breathing, HR <100): suction the mouth and trachea before stimulating the baby to breathe. Routine intubation and suction of all meconium-stained babies is no longer recommended — only suction if the baby is depressed.

Nuchal Cord

The umbilical cord is wrapped around the baby's neck (occurs in 15–25% of deliveries). Management: as the head delivers, gently slip your finger under the cord and attempt to reduce it over the baby's head. If the cord is too tight to reduce, clamp the cord in two places and cut between the clamps, then unwrap the cord from the neck. Cutting the cord before the body delivers is safe because the clamping prevents blood loss — but it interrupts fetal blood flow from the placenta, so the baby must be delivered quickly after cutting.

Postpartum Hemorrhage

Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) is the most common cause of maternal mortality worldwide. It is defined as blood loss >500 mL after vaginal delivery or >1,000 mL after cesarean delivery. The most common cause is uterine atony (failure of the uterus to contract after delivery). Other causes include retained placental fragments, genital tract lacerations, and coagulopathy. Signs: persistent heavy vaginal bleeding, boggy (soft, non-contracted) uterus on palpation, tachycardia, hypotension, and pallor. Management:

  • Uterine (fundal) massage: Place one hand on the lower abdomen just above the pubic symphysis and the other hand on the fundus. Massage the uterus in a circular motion or compress it between both hands until it becomes firm. This stimulates uterine contraction.
  • Oxytocin: If in scope per protocol, administer oxytocin (Pitocin) IM or IV to promote uterine contraction. Oxytocin is the first-line medication for PPH.
  • Transport: PPH can be rapidly fatal. Provide high-flow oxygen, establish IV access, monitor vital signs closely, and transport emergently. Continue fundal massage en route.
  • Do NOT pack the vagina with gauze — this can conceal bleeding and make assessment unreliable.
Critical Warning — Postpartum Hemorrhage: A "boggy" uterus (soft like dough) that does not firm up with massage indicates uterine atony. This is the #1 cause of PPH. Aggressive fundal massage, oxytocin administration per protocol, and rapid transport are life-saving. A mother can lose 1 liter of blood in minutes from an atonic uterus.

Preeclampsia & Eclampsia

Preeclampsia is a pregnancy-specific syndrome of hypertension, proteinuria, and edema developing after 20 weeks of gestation. It affects 5–8% of pregnancies. The cause is not fully understood but involves abnormal placental implantation and systemic endothelial dysfunction. Eclampsia is the progression of preeclampsia to seizure activity (grand mal seizures) that cannot be attributed to other causes. Preeclampsia is diagnosed by:

  • Hypertension: BP ≥140/90 mmHg on two occasions, 4+ hours apart (severe: ≥160/110)
  • Proteinuria: ≥300 mg protein in a 24-hour urine collection
  • Edema: Generalized, especially facial and hand edema (nondependent)
  • Headache: Often severe, frontal, and unresponsive to acetaminophen
  • Visual disturbances: Blurred vision, scotomata (flashing lights or spots), photophobia
  • Epigastric or RUQ pain: Indicates hepatic involvement (HELLP syndrome — Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets)

Eclamptic seizure management: Protect the airway (position lateral recumbent), administer oxygen, protect from injury (pad surroundings), check blood glucose, monitor for postictal state. ALS may administer magnesium sulfate (MgSO₄) — the standard medication for seizure prophylaxis and treatment in eclampsia. Left lateral positioning improves placental perfusion and reduces IVC compression. Transport emergently — eclampsia is a life-threatening emergency for both mother and fetus.

Emergency Childbirth Kit Contents

An EMS provider should be familiar with the contents of an OB delivery kit. Common items include: sterile gloves (multiple pairs), sterile towels and drapes, umbilical cord clamps (2) or hemostats, sterile scissors or scalpel for cord cutting, bulb syringe (for suctioning the newborn's mouth and nose), sterile gauze pads, plastic bag for the placenta, and a hat or cap for the newborn (to prevent heat loss). Many kits also include a disposable blanket for wrapping the newborn, sanitary pads for the mother, and identification bands. In addition to the OB kit, prepare: a warm environment (crank the heat in the ambulance), extra towels for drying and warming the baby, and oxygen equipment with a neonatal mask.

quiz.q: A 28-year-old female at 38 weeks gestation is in active labor. She reports her contractions are 2 minutes apart and she feels an urge to push. On examination, you see the baby's head crowning. What should the EMS provider do?
options: Immediately transport with the mother supine to the hospital for delivery
Prepare for field delivery — stay and deliver since birth is imminent; position the mother, establish a sterile field, and assist with delivery
Apply fundal pressure to slow the delivery so you can reach the hospital
Tell the mother to cross her legs and not push until you reach the hospital
quiz.explanation: When delivery is imminent (crowning, urge to push with contractions 1–2 min apart), the EMS provider should stay and deliver. Never delay transport for delivery unless birth is imminent — but once crowning is visible, delivery will occur before you reach the hospital.

quiz.q: A 32-year-old female at 34 weeks gestation complains of severe headache, blurred vision, and epigastric pain. Her blood pressure is 172/108 mmHg. She has significant facial and hand edema. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: Preeclampsia with severe features — this patient is at high risk for eclampsia (seizures)
quiz.explanation: Severe hypertension (≥160/110), headache, visual changes, epigastric pain, and edema in a pregnant patient after 20 weeks is preeclampsia with severe features until proven otherwise. This patient is at high risk for eclamptic seizures. Place on left side, provide oxygen, reduce stimulation, and transport emergently.

quiz.q: You have just delivered a full-term newborn. The baby is pink, has a heart rate of 120, cries strongly when stimulated, has active motion of all extremities, and is breathing vigorously. What is this baby's APGAR score at 1 minute?
quiz.explanation: This baby scores 2 in every category: Appearance (2 — pink all over), Pulse (2 — ≥100), Grimace (2 — strong cry), Activity (2 — active motion), Respiration (2 — vigorous cry/breathing). Total = 10. APGAR 7–10 is reassuring.

quiz.q: [HARD] A 26-year-old female delivers a baby with thick, green-tinged amniotic fluid. The baby is limp, cyanotic, and has a heart rate of 80. What is the priority intervention?
options: Immediately stimulate the baby by rubbing the back vigorously
Begin positive-pressure ventilation (PPV) — for a non-vigorous meconium-stained newborn, routine tracheal suctioning is no longer recommended; clear the mouth and nose only as needed
Start positive pressure ventilation immediately — the baby needs oxygen
Begin chest compressions because the heart rate is below 100
quiz.explanation: Thick meconium staining with a depressed baby (poor tone, HR <100, cyanosis) requires oropharyngeal and tracheal suctioning BEFORE stimulation to prevent meconium aspiration. Do NOT stimulate the baby until the airway is cleared — stimulation triggers gasping and can pull meconium into the lungs.

flashcards.question: What is supine hypotensive syndrome and how is it managed?
flashcards.answer: Compression of the IVC by the gravid uterus when a pregnant patient (>20 weeks) lies supine, reducing venous return and causing hypotension. Management: roll the patient onto their LEFT side or tilt the pelvis 15–30° to displace the uterus off the IVC.

flashcards.question: What are the four stages of labor?
flashcards.answer: 1st: Cervical dilation (0–10 cm). 2nd: Delivery of the baby. 3rd: Delivery of the placenta. 4th: Immediate postpartum recovery and monitoring.

flashcards.question: What are the five components of the APGAR score and what is the minimum score that requires intervention?
flashcards.answer: Appearance (skin color), Pulse (heart rate), Grimace (reflex irritability), Activity (muscle tone), Respiration. Each scored 0–2. Total 0–10. A score <7 at 1 minute requires intervention (stimulate, warm, suction, oxygen).

flashcards.question: What is the correct sequence for suctioning a newborn?
flashcards.answer: Suction the MOUTH first, then the NOSE. Suctioning the mouth first prevents aspiration if the baby gasps when the nose is stimulated. Use a bulb syringe and suction gently to avoid vagal stimulation and bradycardia.

flashcards.question: How should an EMS provider manage a prolapsed umbilical cord?
flashcards.answer: Place the mother in knee-chest position (or extreme Trendelenburg), insert a gloved hand to elevate the presenting part off the cord with two fingers, keep the cord moist and warm with saline-soaked gauze, and transport emergently for cesarean delivery.

flashcards.question: What is the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage and what is the EMS provider's primary intervention?
flashcards.answer: Uterine atony (failure of the uterus to contract after delivery). Management: fundal (uterine) massage to stimulate contraction, administer oxytocin per protocol, high-flow oxygen, IV access, and rapid transport. PPH is the leading cause of maternal mortality.

flashcards.question: What are the signs of placental separation?
flashcards.answer: A gush of blood from the vagina, lengthening of the umbilical cord, and the uterus becoming firm and globular (rising in the abdomen). Do NOT pull on the cord — allow spontaneous delivery.

flashcards.question: How does an EMS provider manage a nuchal cord?
flashcards.answer: Gently attempt to reduce the cord over the baby's head. If too tight, clamp the cord in two places and cut between the clamps, then unwrap from the baby's neck. The baby must be delivered quickly after cutting.

critical.scenario: You are called to a 30-year-old female at 39 weeks gestation who reports her 'water broke' 4 hours ago and she has been having regular contractions. She is now feeling the urge to push. On examination, you see the baby's head crowning with each contraction. The mother is anxious and asks you to 'make sure my baby is okay.' You set up for field delivery. As the head delivers, you slide your finger along the baby's neck and feel the umbilical cord wrapped tightly around it twice. The cord is too tight to reduce over the head.
critical.question: What is your immediate action for managing this nuchal cord, and what are the next steps in delivery?

options.t: Clamp the cord in two places 2–3 inches apart and cut between the clamps to release the loops, then deliver the baby without delay
options.f: Correct — A tight nuchal cord that cannot be reduced over the head requires clamping, cutting, and immediate delivery. After cutting, the anterior shoulder is delivered by guiding the head downward (toward the mother's sacrum), then the posterior shoulder by guiding the head upward. Suction mouth then nose after delivery of the head. Dry, warm, and stimulate the baby after full delivery.

options.t: Transport the mother immediately with the baby's head partially delivered — the hospital can cut the cord
options.f: Incorrect — You cannot transport a mother with the baby's head delivered and the body still inside. The baby's face is compressed against the perineum, the cord may tighten further, and the baby cannot breathe. You must complete the delivery in the field.

critical.explanation: A tight nuchal cord prevents further delivery and can strangle the baby. The cord must be clamped, cut, and the baby delivered. After delivery, perform routine care: dry, warm, stimulate, suction, and APGAR scoring. Document the presence and management of the nuchal cord. Transport both mother and baby (with baby skin-to-skin or in a warm blanket) for ongoing care.
critical.kpi: Nuchal cord management — clamping, cutting, and delivery sequence

critical.scenario: You are transporting a 34-year-old female who delivered a healthy 7 lb 2 oz baby boy 10 minutes ago in the field. The placenta has not yet delivered. The mother is now complaining of 'really bad cramps' and you notice a large amount of bright red blood soaking through the towels under her. Her uterus feels soft and boggy on palpation — it does not firm up when you attempt to massage it. Her vital signs: HR 124, BP 96/62, RR 22, SpO₂ 97%. She is pale and diaphoretic.
critical.question: What is the most likely diagnosis and what are your priority interventions?

options.t: This is normal postpartum bleeding — up to 500 mL is expected. The boggy uterus is normal as the placenta has not yet delivered. Continue monitoring and transport routinely.
options.f: Incorrect — A boggy uterus that does not firm with massage is uterine atony, the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage. The tachycardia, borderline hypotension, pallor, and diaphoresis indicate significant blood loss. This is a life-threatening emergency.

options.t: Postpartum hemorrhage from uterine atony — begin aggressive fundal massage with both hands (one on the fundus, one supporting the lower uterus), administer oxytocin per protocol, apply high-flow oxygen, establish IV access, monitor vital signs every 5 minutes, and transport emergently with lights and sirens
options.f: Correct — Uterine atony is the #1 cause of PPH. The uterus must be massaged until firm to control bleeding. Oxytocin promotes sustained uterine contraction. This patient is showing signs of hemorrhagic shock (tachycardia, hypotension, pallor). Rapid transport is essential.

critical.explanation: Postpartum hemorrhage from uterine atony is a leading cause of maternal mortality. The boggy uterus that does not respond to massage is the hallmark sign. Aggressive fundal massage, oxytocin administration, and rapid transport are life-saving. Do NOT pack the vagina. Monitor for signs of worsening shock. The placenta will deliver spontaneously — do not pull on the cord to retrieve it. Notify the receiving hospital of a PPH in progress.
critical.kpi: Recognises postpartum hemorrhage from uterine atony with appropriate interventions

critical.scenario: You respond to a 22-year-old primigravida (first pregnancy) at 32 weeks gestation who reports a sudden onset of severe headache and blurred vision. Her husband states she has been 'swollen all over' for the past week and has gained 10 pounds in the last month. Her blood pressure is 178/114 mmHg. She is confused, agitated, and has been vomiting. As you attempt to move her to the stretcher, she suddenly stiffens and begins having generalized tonic-clonic seizure activity.
critical.question: What is this condition and what are your management priorities?

options.t: The patient is having a panic attack from the stress of pregnancy — provide calm reassurance and transport
options.f: Incorrect — Severe hypertension (178/114), headache, visual changes, generalized edema, and new-onset seizure in a pregnant patient after 20 weeks is eclampsia until proven otherwise. This is a life-threatening emergency for both mother and fetus.

options.t: Eclampsia — protect the airway by positioning the mother on her LEFT side, clear hazards, time the seizure, administer oxygen, check blood glucose, provide suction if needed, and transport emergently. ALS may administer magnesium sulfate (MgSO₄) for seizure control
options.f: Correct — Eclamptic seizures result from severe preeclampsia progressing to CNS irritability. Key management: left lateral positioning (improves placental perfusion, reduces aspiration risk), oxygen, seizure precautions, and rapid transport. Magnesium sulfate is the first-line treatment for eclampsia.

critical.explanation: Preeclampsia with severe features (BP ≥160/110, severe headache, visual changes, edema, epigastric pain) has progressed to eclampsia (seizure). This is a high-risk obstetric emergency. Left lateral positioning is essential to improve uterine blood flow and reduce aspiration risk. Do not restrain the seizure. Monitor fetal status if possible (ask the mother if she feels fetal movement after the seizure). Transport emergently — both mother and baby are at risk of significant morbidity.
critical.kpi: Recognises eclampsia and implements appropriate seizure management
